name: Tour of the Cazorla and Las Villas Sierras
name_original: Subida a Mirador Tapadero y Sierra de las Villas — Parque Natural Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas
description: This difficult 94.4-mile road cycling route ascends to Mirador Tapadero, offering stunning mountain views in Parque Natural Sierras de Cazor
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Santiago-Pontones traverse the rugged terrain of the Sierra de Segura, characterized by significant elevation changes and winding mountain roads. The region features deep valleys, dense pine forests, and numerous reservoirs such as Embalse del Tranco de Beas and Embalse del Anchuricas. Road cyclists can expect challenging climbs and descents, often leading to scenic viewpoints over the surrounding natural park. This area provides a demanding environment for road cycling, with routes that frequently include passes and panoramic vistas. Best road cycling routes around Santiago-Pontones The most popular road cycling route is Puerto Despiernacaballo y Mirador de Huelguecillas — circular por Hornos, a 49.8 miles (80.1 km) trail that takes 4 hours 30 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant climbing and panoramic views from Mirador de Huelguecillas. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Tour of the Cazorla and Las Villas Sierras, a difficult 94.4 miles (151.9 km) path. This extensive route explores the diverse landscapes of the Cazorla and Las Villas mountain ranges. Local road cyclists also love the Embalse del Anchuricas by the Mirador de La Novia — circular by the Sierra de la Graña, a 35.5 miles (57.1 km) trail leading through mountainous terrain and past the Embalse del Anchuricas, often completed in about 3 hours 15 minutes. Road cycling around Santiago-Pontones is defined by challenging mountain passes, deep valleys, and scenic viewpoints over reservoirs. The network offers options primarily for experienced road cyclists, with numerous advanced road bike trails Santiago-Pontones climbs. The routes in Santiago-Pontones are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from more than 5 reviews. More than 900 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Santiago-Pontones's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available in Santiago-Pontones?
A: There are over 35 road cycling routes around Santiago-Pontones, offering a variety of challenges primarily for experienced cyclists. The region's rugged terrain means most routes feature significant elevation changes.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly road cycling routes in Santiago-Pontones?
A: The road cycling routes in Santiago-Pontones are generally challenging, characterized by significant elevation changes and winding mountain roads. There are no routes classified as 'easy' in the area, with 13 routes considered 'moderate' and 26 as 'difficult'. Beginners should be prepared for demanding climbs and descents.

Q: What kind of climbs can I expect on advanced road bike trails in Santiago-Pontones?
A: Advanced road bike trails in Santiago-Pontones feature demanding mountain passes and substantial elevation gains. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1276016024/tour-of-the-cazorla-and-las-villas-sierras" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tour of the Cazorla and Las Villas Sierras&lt;/a&gt; route includes over 3,100 meters of climbing over its 151.9 km distance, offering a true test for experienced riders.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in Santiago-Pontones?
A: Yes, many routes in Santiago-Pontones are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1277348970/puerto-despiernacaballo-y-mirador-de-huelguecillas-circular-por-hornos" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto Despiernacaballo y Mirador de Huelguecillas — circular por Hornos&lt;/a&gt;, which is 80.1 km long and offers panoramic views.

Q: What are the typical lengths and durations of road cycling routes in this region?
A: Route lengths vary, but many are designed for longer rides. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1277356362/embalse-del-anchuricas-by-the-mirador-de-la-novia-circular-by-the-sierra-de-la-grana" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse del Anchuricas by the Mirador de La Novia — circular by the Sierra de la Graña&lt;/a&gt; is 57.1 km and typically takes around 3 hours 15 minutes. Longer routes, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1276016024/tour-of-the-cazorla-and-las-villas-sierras" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tour of the Cazorla and Las Villas Sierras&lt;/a&gt;, can extend to over 150 km and require more than 8 hours to complete.

Q: What scenic viewpoints can I expect along the road cycling routes?
A: The mountainous terrain of Santiago-Pontones offers numerous scenic viewpoints. Routes often lead to panoramic vistas over deep valleys and reservoirs. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1345001169/embalse-del-tranco-de-beas-and-ascent-to-mirador-de-huelguecillas-circular-from-hornos" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse del Tranco de Beas and ascent to Mirador de Huelguecillas — circular from Hornos&lt;/a&gt; route, for example, features the stunning &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3896738" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Isla Cabeza de la Viña Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; and the Mirador de Huelguecillas.

Q: Are there any lakes or waterfalls to see while road cycling in Santiago-Pontones?
A: Yes, the region is home to several beautiful water features. You can cycle past reservoirs like Embalse del Tranco de Beas and Embalse del Anchuricas. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1277356362/embalse-del-anchuricas-by-the-mirador-de-la-novia-circular-by-the-sierra-de-la-grana" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse del Anchuricas by the Mirador de La Novia — circular by the Sierra de la Graña&lt;/a&gt; route passes the Embalse del Anchuricas. Additionally, you might encounter natural attractions such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6598554" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Calavera Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3168204" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Charco del Aceite&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Santiago-Pontones?
A: The komoot community highly rates road cycling in Santiago-Pontones, with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 5 reviews. Cyclists often praise the challenging mountain passes, the deep valleys, and the scenic viewpoints over the region's reservoirs, highlighting the demanding yet rewarding environment for road cycling.

Q: What is the best time of year for road biking in Santiago-Pontones?
A: Given the region's mountainous characteristics, spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant conditions for road cycling, with milder temperatures. Summers can be very hot, and winters can bring cold temperatures and potentially snow at higher elevations, making cycling more challenging.

Q: Are there any less crowded road bike routes in the Sierra de Segura area?
A: While specific crowd levels can vary, the Sierra de Segura region, including Santiago-Pontones, is known for its rugged and expansive natural park. Many routes traverse remote areas, offering a sense of solitude away from major tourist hubs. Exploring routes that venture deeper into the natural park, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1345010776/ascent-to-el-yelmo-circular-route-around-the-tranco-reservoir" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ascent to El Yelmo — circular route around the Tranco Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;, might provide a less crowded experience.

Q: Are there places to stop for refreshments or cafes along the road cycling routes?
A: While the routes often pass through remote natural areas, some villages and towns along the way may offer opportunities for refreshments. It's advisable to plan your route and check for available services in advance, especially on longer rides, and carry sufficient water and snacks as options can be sparse in the more isolated sections.
tips: The Tranco De Beas reservoir is located on the Guadalquivir river in the center of the Sierra de Castril Natural Park. The reservoir has a hydroelectric dam that supplies the grid with sustainable energy.

Ascent in the form of a snake from the Guadalquivir river towards the Tapadero viewpoint. Total distance of 8.87 km gaining 609 m of unevenness up to 1,144 m with an average slope of 6.1%. to reach spectacular views.
Beautiful road that goes through one of the mountains in the Sierra de Cazorla, with an asphalted road and always with impressive views of the area as a whole!

Santo Tomé is located on the slopes of the Sierra de Cazorla, in the valley of the Guadalquivir river. passing the center of the village you can see the bell tower of the stone church, dating from the fourteenth century.

This is the most characteristic point from which you can see the entire valley of the Guadalquivir River, and all the human settlements that currently exist such as Arroyo Frío, El Chaparral or Loma de María Ángela. 
It is located at an altitude of 1241 meters above sea level.

faqs.question: What is the overall difficulty of this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is categorized as a difficult road ride, demanding very good fitness due to its significant length of approximately 152 kilometers and an impressive 3,110 meters of elevation gain. Cyclists should be prepared for continuous climbs and descents through mountainous landscapes.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on the Ascent to Mirador Tapadero and Sierra de las Villas?
faqs.answer: The route is mostly well-paved, typical for road cycling. However, be aware that some segments may be unpaved and could be challenging to navigate on a road bike. This mix of surfaces contributes to the route's high difficulty rating.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along this cycling tour?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter several notable spots, including the scenic Tranco de Beas Reservoir, the rewarding Tapadero Viewpoint, and the challenging Sierra de las Villas Road. Other highlights include the Church of Santo Tomé and the panoramic Puerto de las Palomas Viewpoint.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route in Parque Natural Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas?
faqs.answer: While the tour can be enjoyed in various seasons, spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant conditions for cycling, with milder temperatures and vibrant natural scenery. Summer can be very hot, and winter may bring colder temperatures and potential for adverse weather in the mountains.

faqs.question: Are there any specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle within the Parque Natural Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for cycling on public roads within the Parque Natural Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas. However, always respect local regulations, especially concerning protected areas and wildlife. It's advisable to check with local tourism offices for any updated information before your trip.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: As this is a demanding road cycling route, bringing dogs along is generally not recommended, especially for their safety and comfort over such a long distance and elevation. If you plan to bring a dog, ensure they are secured and that local regulations regarding pets in the natural park are followed, particularly in protected areas.

faqs.question: What kind of wildlife might I see while cycling through the park?
faqs.answer: The Parque Natural Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas is rich in wildlife. You might spot fallow deer, foxes, and wild boar. Birdwatchers can look out for impressive species such as bearded vultures, falcons, and eagles soaring above the spectacular mountain scenery.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with any other well-known trails or cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route intersects with several other paths. You'll find sections that overlap with official trails such as [CIMA JA02] Collado de la Traviesa (Pocico), [CIMA JA01] Las Palomas * Cazorla ~ Hilario Marcos, and [CIMA JA07] Collado Agua de los Perros. It also briefly touches parts of the Transandalus and Los Galdones trails.

faqs.question: Where can I find parking to access the Ascent to Mirador Tapadero and Sierra de las Villas route?
faqs.answer: The komoot tour description does not specify a precise starting point or designated parking. Given the length of the route, it likely starts and ends in a town or village within or near the Parque Natural Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas. It's recommended to research parking options in nearby towns like Cazorla or La Iruela, or other access points within the park, depending on where you plan to begin your ride.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or places to refuel along the route?
faqs.answer: Given the route's significant length and remote sections within the natural park, opportunities for cafes or refueling might be limited. It's crucial to carry sufficient water, snacks, and any necessary supplies. Plan your stops carefully, perhaps in the larger villages or towns you might pass through, or consider packing enough to be self-sufficient for the duration of your ride.

faqs.question: What makes the scenery on this route particularly special?
faqs.answer: The route traverses the Parque Natural Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas, a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve known for its spectacular mountain scenery. You'll experience deep, forested ranges, unique karst formations, and forests of holly, hazel, and ferns. The landscape features mountains that witnessed glacier formation, gorges sculpted by rivers, incredible waterfalls, and crystal-clear water pools, offering truly eye-searing beauty.
